title: Heights of Gross-Schoen and Ceresa Cycles
speaker: Ziyang Gao
abstract:
In this talk, we construct a Zariski open dense locus in Mg
 on which the Beilinson-Bloch height of the Gross-Schoen and Ceresa cycles is a Weil height, i.e. it has a lower bound and satisfies the Northcott property. This implies a generic positivity result of the heights of these cycles. This is joint work with Shouwu Zhang.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Height-theoretic template for torsion and pairings
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Chow-group questions about detecting torsion and perfect pairings, recalls dominant heights with lower bounds and Northcott property, and frames Beilinson-Bloch heights as the conjectural extension to homologically trivial cycles.

proof_stages.label: Gross-Schoen/Ceresa cycle problem on moduli of curves
proof_stages.summary: For curves of genus at least two the Gross-Schoen modified diagonal and Ceresa cycle are introduced, their torsion and height behavior is related, and the main generic-positivity theorem is formulated as dominance of the Gross-Schoen height on a maximal nonempty Q-defined Zariski open locus in M_g.

proof_stages.label: Adelic line bundle realizing the Gross-Schoen height
proof_stages.summary: The arithmetic height is converted into the height associated with an adelic metrized line bundle on integral models of M_g, constructed using the universal Jacobian, the Poincare line bundle, and the power dynamical system on abelian schemes.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to bigness and restricted-volume loci
proof_stages.summary: Dominance of the height on a dense open is reduced to positivity of the volume of the generic-fiber line bundle and to understanding the subvarieties on which its restricted volume vanishes, which also governs descent of the open locus over Q.

proof_stages.label: Volume identity despite unknown nefness
proof_stages.summary: A key analytic-arithmetic theorem expresses the volume as the integral of the top exterior power of the curvature form, proved directly without nefness by adelic approximation, fast convergence from torsion-point averaging, and holomorphic Morse inequalities.

proof_stages.label: Betti form interpretation of the curvature
proof_stages.summary: The curvature form is identified with the Betti form attached to the normal-function/Abel-Jacobi section; via intermediate Jacobians, local Betti maps, and Griffiths transversality this form is semipositive and its top wedge is controlled by intersections with Betti leaves.

proof_stages.label: Algebraicity and nonvanishing of Betti strata
proof_stages.summary: O-minimality, Ax-Schanuel, and geometries over period maps are used to prove the relevant Betti stratum is Zariski closed and to give a criterion for it to be all of the base, yielding the needed semipositivity, nonzero top wedge, and closed vanishing locus for M_g.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and remaining refinements
proof_stages.summary: After identifying the dominant-height locus as the complement of the Betti stratum, the talk lists remaining problems: computing this locus explicitly, proving nefness and nonnegativity of the adelic line bundle, and ruling out torsion Ceresa cycles at algebraic points in the locus.

examples.name: Classical Weil height on projective space
examples.note: The logarithmic height of rational/algebraic points on projective space illustrates the lower-bound and Northcott properties that define the height-theoretic template for the later cycle heights.
examples.engagement_reason: He defines the Weil height by explicit formulas over rationals and number fields, explains normalization, well-definedness via the product formula, and derives basic properties such as nonnegativity, Northcott, and the zero-height points.

passes.reason: Projective space is a maximally homogeneous setting for the standard height, used as a motivating positive model.

Used as the prototype positive height with Northcott/lower-bound properties; projective space is the maximally symmetric ambient variety.

Used as the basic positive model of a height, with projective space a maximally homogeneous object.

examples.name: Néron-Tate height on an abelian variety
examples.note: The canonical height attached to a symmetric ample line bundle on an abelian variety exemplifies a genuine nonnegative height whose zero set is exactly the torsion subgroup.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the abelian-variety height from a symmetric ample line bundle and the multiplication-by-two dynamical system, then uses it to identify torsion vanishing and a positive definite quadratic pairing.

passes.reason: The canonical height on a general abelian variety is presented as a standard positive example, not as a refutation.
The Néron-Tate height is presented as a canonical positive height on a general polarized abelian variety, not as an adversarial or sporadic case.
The Néron–Tate height is presented as a standard canonical height on abelian varieties giving a positive quadratic form modulo torsion.
Presented as a canonical dominant height giving a positive quadratic form on an abelian variety, not as an obstruction or sporadic case.
the Néron–Tate height is used as a standard canonical height on abelian varieties with torsion as its zero locus, not to refute anything.
The Néron–Tate height is presented as a standard canonical height on abelian varieties with torsion as its zero locus, not as an obstruction.
It is presented as a positive paradigm of a canonical height and pairing on abelian varieties, with no sporadic or boundary structure invoked.
Cited as a standard positive model of a canonical height on abelian varieties, not as an obstruction or sporadic case.
It is a canonical height on a general abelian variety, used as a positive model for a height pairing.
Used as another standard positive height example on abelian varieties, not to refute a claim and not as a sporadic or limiting case.
The Néron-Tate height is a canonical quadratic height from the regular group/polarization structure of an abelian variety, used positively.
Used as a positive example of a dominant/canonical height on a systematic family of abelian varieties.

examples.name: Faltings height of Jacobians
examples.note: The Faltings height of the Jacobian of a curve is used as another model of a dominant height function on a moduli space, motivating the attempt to put cycle heights on moduli.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites Faltings height of Jacobians as a dominant height function on moduli used in Faltings’ proof, but does not compute or construct it in detail.

passes.reason: Faltings height of Jacobians is used as another standard dominant-height example in arithmetic geometry.
Faltings height of Jacobians is an ordinary height on an infinite moduli family used as a motivating example.

Used as another standard dominant height example in the proof of Mordell, with no exceptional or adversarial role.
the Faltings height of Jacobians is cited as a standard dominant height used in Faltings’ proof, with no sporadic or limiting structure.

examples.name: Hyperelliptic curves as a zero-height locus
examples.note: Hyperelliptic curves have vanishing Gross-Schoen/Ceresa height, and this obstruction visibly forces the formulation to move from all of M_g to a dense open locus.
examples.engagement_reason: He states that the Gross-Schoen/Ceresa heights vanish on the hyperelliptic locus, referring to the involution, but does not work through the verification on a hyperelliptic curve.

passes.reason: Hyperelliptic curves form the minimal-gonality zero-height locus and are used to show the height cannot satisfy Northcott on all of Mg.
The hyperelliptic locus is a zero-height locus and is used to show Northcott dominance fails on all of M_g.
Hyperelliptic curves form a vanishing, zero-height locus and are used to show the height cannot satisfy Northcott on all of M_g.
The hyperelliptic locus is a zero-height/vanishing locus and is used to show the height cannot satisfy Northcott on all of Mg.
the hyperelliptic locus is a height-vanishing extremal locus and is used to show the Gross–Schoen/Ceresa height cannot have Northcott on all of moduli.
Hyperelliptic curves form a special minimum/zero-height locus and are used to show the Gross–Schoen/Ceresa height cannot have Northcott on all of Mg.
Hyperelliptic curves form the minimal-gonality locus and are used because their zero height prevents Northcott/dominance on all of Mg.
The hyperelliptic locus has minimal gonality and height zero, and is used to show the Gross-Schoen/Ceresa height cannot be a Northcott height on all of M_g.
The hyperelliptic locus is used to show the height is not dominant on all of M_g, and hyperelliptic curves have minimal possible gonality 2.
The hyperelliptic locus has height zero and is used to show Northcott cannot hold on all of moduli.
Hyperelliptic curves form the zero-height obstruction to Northcott on all of Mg and are structurally extremal as minimal-gonality curves.
Used to show the height cannot be dominant on all of moduli, and hyperelliptic curves are the minimal-gonality edge of the curve class.

examples.name: Degree-zero point on a curve and the Abel-Jacobi map
examples.note: The familiar map from a point minus a base point on a curve to its Jacobian is used to make the higher intermediate-Jacobian Abel-Jacobi construction concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He concretely explains the curve case of Abel-Jacobi by choosing a base point, forming the degree-zero cycle point minus base point, and mapping it to the Jacobian/intermediate Jacobian.

passes.reason: A degree-zero divisor p minus a base point is the ordinary illustrative input for the Abel-Jacobi map.
A divisor p-p0 on a curve is the minimal curve-level Abel-Jacobi base case used only for illustration.
A point minus a chosen base point is the classical ordinary Abel–Jacobi example for curves, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
A degree-zero point minus a base point is the ordinary curve-level illustration of the Abel-Jacobi map, not a counterexample.
